item.title: The Last Sunday in June: How Pride Became America's Great Public Artwork
item.body:
Every year, as June winds down, American cities erupt in color. The last Sunday of the month brings some of the country's largest Pride marches — New York's among them — capping a month of parades, exhibitions, and performances. Strip away the politics for a moment and what remains is one of the most ambitious pieces of public art the country produces annually: a city-scale collaboration made by hundreds of thousands of people, all at once.

## Born from a single street

The tradition traces back to the summer of 1969 and the uprising at the Stonewall Inn in Greenwich Village. The first marches, a year later, were small and defiant. More than five decades on, they've grown into sprawling civic spectacles — but the DNA of that original street protest still runs underneath the confetti.

## An art form of its own

Look at a Pride march as choreography and design and it reveals real craft:

- **Costume and color.** Months of making go into a single afternoon of movement — a democratized fashion show with no gatekeepers.
- **Sound as architecture.** Marching bands, sound systems, and drum lines turn a boulevard into a moving concert hall.
- **The float as sculpture.** Community groups build kinetic, temporary monuments that exist for a few hours and then vanish.

## Why it still matters as culture

Museums spend months curating shows that a few thousand people will see. A major Pride march reaches millions in a single day, in the open air, for free. It's culture that refuses the frame on the wall — participatory, ephemeral, impossible to own.

## Beyond the parade

The best way to experience the season is to look past the headline event: the gallery retrospectives, the archival film screenings, the small readings and drag performances tucked into neighborhood venues. That's where the history lives, and where the art gets personal.

As the month closes, the streets clear, the color fades — and the city goes back to normal. But for one Sunday, an entire metropolis becomes the canvas.
